name: Knewton
summary: Pioneer of AI-driven adaptive learning platforms for higher-ed courseware; raised huge rounds on the promise of personalized learning paths but never found a durable business model and was sold to Wiley for a fraction of capital raised.
lesson: Being first to define a category (adaptive learning) does not guarantee winning it; without a clear path to profitable unit economics, massive funding just delays the reckoning.
source: Wiley 8-K acquisition filing 2019; EdSurge coverage of Knewton sale

name: Smart Sparrow
summary: Australian adaptive courseware platform used by universities to build personalized, data-driven lessons; shut down operations in 2020 after failing to scale beyond a niche higher-ed customer base.
lesson: Adaptive learning tools sold into university IT/LMS procurement cycles face brutally long sales cycles that can starve a startup of growth before product value compounds.
source: EdSurge shutdown coverage 2020; Smart Sparrow customer notices

name: Declara
summary: AI-powered social learning and knowledge-recommendation platform backed by high-profile advisors including Nelson Mandela's foundation; collapsed amid founder disputes and allegations over use of Mandela's name, followed by abrupt shutdown.
lesson: Prestige partnerships and advisor names can accelerate fundraising but create governance and reputational risk if the underlying relationship is mismanaged.
source: TechCrunch/EdSurge reporting on Declara controversy and closure 2018

name: Fishtree
summary: Personalized-learning platform that used AI to recommend curriculum content to K-12 teachers; ceased operations after failing to convert pilots into sustained district contracts.
lesson: K-12 pilots that impress teachers do not automatically convert to district-wide paid deployments; procurement complexity kills many promising adaptive-learning tools.
source: EdSurge coverage of Fishtree closure

name: Yup (MathCrunch)
summary: AI-assisted, human-in-the-loop math and science tutoring app acquired by Byju's in 2020; shut down in 2023 as Byju's slashed its US portfolio amid its own financial collapse.
lesson: Being acquired by a cash-rich parent is not safety — when the parent's own balance sheet implodes, acquired subsidiaries get cut regardless of their standalone traction.
source: TechCrunch reporting on Byju's US shutdowns 2023

name: Thinkster Math
summary: AI-personalized math tutoring app blending algorithmic diagnostics with human tutors for K-8 students; wound down operations after struggling to scale customer acquisition profitably in a crowded direct-to-consumer tutoring market.
lesson: Consumer-subscription tutoring apps face brutal paid-acquisition costs; without a defensible retention loop, growth spend simply buys churn.
source: Crunchbase company status; EdSurge consumer edtech coverage

name: Numerade
summary: AI and video-based STEM homework help platform that raised a large Series B at a unicorn-adjacent valuation during the pandemic edtech boom, then conducted major layoffs and pivots as post-pandemic demand and free ChatGPT-based alternatives eroded its market.
lesson: Pandemic-era edtech valuations priced in growth rates that reverted sharply once schools reopened and generative AI chatbots became free substitutes for paid homework-help products.
source: TechCrunch/Forbes coverage of Numerade layoffs 2023

name: Ello
summary: AI-powered reading tutor app for young children using speech recognition to listen to kids read aloud and adapt instruction; shut down in 2023 after failing to reach sustainable scale.
lesson: Speech-recognition-based literacy tutoring for young children requires very high accuracy to earn parent trust; technical shortfalls in noisy home environments undermine retention.
source: Company shutdown announcement 2023; EdTech Insiders coverage

name: Century Tech
summary: UK-based AI adaptive learning platform for schools built with neuroscience and cognitive-science backing; entered administration/restructuring in 2023 after years of cash burn without reaching profitability in the UK schools market.
lesson: Selling AI adaptive learning into cash-strapped state school budgets caps achievable price points, making it hard to outrun burn even with strong pedagogical credibility.
source: Companies House filings; UK EdTech press on Century Tech restructuring 2023

name: Kidaptive
summary: AI-driven adaptive learning platform for young children (Leo's Pad) that pivoted repeatedly from consumer app to a learner-analytics licensing business before quietly winding down.
lesson: Repeated business-model pivots (consumer app to B2B analytics) burn both cash and team focus; each pivot needs to fully validate demand before the next reinvention.
source: Crunchbase; EdSurge historical coverage of Kidaptive pivots

name: VIPKid
summary: Once a $4.5B-valued AI-matched online English tutoring marketplace connecting Chinese students with North American teachers; collapsed after China's 2021 ban on for-profit after-school tutoring and foreign-teacher restrictions eliminated its core market almost overnight.
lesson: Regulatory risk in a single-country market can erase a business model overnight; heavy geographic concentration is an existential risk regardless of AI-matching technology quality.
source: Reuters/Bloomberg coverage of China's 2021 tutoring crackdown and VIPKid layoffs

name: Zuoyebang
summary: Baidu-backed AI homework-help and adaptive tutoring app that reached a ~$10B valuation before China's 2021 'double reduction' policy banned for-profit K-12 tutoring, forcing mass layoffs and a pivot toward adult and vocational education.
lesson: Massive AI-education valuations built on a single regulatory regime can vaporize instantly when that government changes policy; diversification of markets is a real hedge, not just a growth tactic.
source: Bloomberg/Reuters reporting on China tutoring crackdown 2021

name: Yuanfudao
summary: China's most valuable AI-tutoring startup (~$15.5B valuation, backed by Tencent and IDG) offering adaptive K-12 tutoring and homework-grading AI; forced into mass layoffs (tens of thousands of jobs cut) after the 2021 for-profit tutoring ban.
lesson: The largest, best-funded company in a regulated sector is not insulated from policy risk — scale amplifies the fall when the rules change.
source: Reuters coverage of Yuanfudao layoffs and valuation collapse 2021

name: Zhangmen Education (Gaotu Techedu)
summary: NYSE-listed Chinese AI-adaptive online tutoring company (formerly Zhangmen, rebranded Gaotu) that lost over 90% of its market value after the 2021 tutoring ban and pivoted its AI personalization technology toward adult and non-academic education.
lesson: Public-market investors in China edtech AI plays learned that even strong AI-personalization technology cannot survive a total ban on the underlying business category.
source: SEC filings (GOTU); Reuters coverage of Gaotu stock collapse 2021

name: Byju's
summary: Once the world's most valuable edtech company ($22B peak valuation), using AI-adaptive learning across its app and acquisitions (Osmo, WhiteHat Jr, Toppr); collapsed into insolvency proceedings by 2023-2025 amid governance scandals, auditor resignations, board exodus, and default on a $1.2B term loan.
lesson: Aggressive debt-fueled acquisition sprees and opaque accounting can destroy even category-defining scale; governance failures compound faster than any product advantage can offset.
source: Financial Times/Reuters coverage of Byju's insolvency 2023-2025; NCLT filings India

name: WhiteHat Jr
summary: AI-personalized coding education platform for children acquired by Byju's in a landmark $300M deal in 2020; shut down entirely in 2023 as part of Byju's broader restructuring and cash crisis, after facing years of criticism over aggressive marketing tactics.
lesson: A high-profile acquisition price is not a validation of sustainable unit economics; when marketing spend outpaces genuine retention, the business is vulnerable once parent-company cash dries up.
source: Economic Times/TechCrunch coverage of WhiteHat Jr shutdown 2023

name: Vedantu
summary: Indian live online tutoring platform using AI-driven personalization (WAVE technology) that reached unicorn status in 2021; conducted repeated rounds of layoffs (over 600 employees across 2022-2023) and shelved its planned IPO as growth reversed post-pandemic.
lesson: Pandemic-driven online tutoring demand was cyclical, not structural; companies that scaled headcount to pandemic-peak demand had to unwind it painfully once classrooms reopened.
source: Economic Times/Moneycontrol coverage of Vedantu layoffs 2022-2023

name: Unacademy
summary: Indian test-prep and AI-personalized learning unicorn (peak valuation ~$3.4B) that conducted five-plus rounds of layoffs between 2022 and 2024, cutting thousands of jobs and shutting several verticals as investors reassessed edtech valuations.
lesson: Rapid multi-vertical expansion (K-12, test prep, upskilling) funded by growth-stage capital creates a large cost base that becomes unsustainable the moment funding markets tighten.
source: Entrackr/Economic Times coverage of Unacademy layoffs 2022-2024

name: Chegg
summary: Publicly-traded homework-help and AI tutoring company (CheggMate, built on GPT-4) that lost over 95% of its market value after 2023 when it disclosed that ChatGPT was directly cannibalizing student demand for its paid Q&A and tutoring services, triggering mass layoffs and strategic review including a possible sale.
lesson: A generative-AI product can become an existential competitor to an incumbent's core business almost instantly; being an early adopter of AI features does not protect a company whose entire value proposition a free general-purpose chatbot can replicate.
source: SEC 8-K filing May 2023 warning on ChatGPT impact; Chegg Q1 2023 earnings call

name: Merlyn Mind
summary: AI voice-assistant and orchestration platform purpose-built for K-12 classrooms, spun out of IBM Watson veterans; faced significant funding and growth struggles by 2024 amid a broader collapse in venture appetite for hardware-adjacent AI-education products.
lesson: Hardware-adjacent AI products for schools carry higher capital intensity (devices, integrations, IT support) than pure software, making them more exposed when follow-on funding tightens.
source: EdWeek/EdSurge coverage of Merlyn Mind funding difficulties 2024

name: Nerdy Inc.
summary: Parent company of Varsity Tutors, which built an AI-powered instructor-matching and adaptive tutoring platform; went public via SPAC at a ~$1.6B valuation in 2021 but its stock subsequently fell more than 90% amid weak profitability and slowing enrollment growth.
lesson: SPAC-era edtech valuations often front-loaded pandemic tutoring demand; public markets punished the mismatch between growth-stage cost structures and normalized post-pandemic revenue.
source: SEC filings (NRDY); public stock price history since 2021 SPAC listing

name: Querium
summary: AI-powered STEM tutoring platform (StepWise) that used machine learning to provide step-by-step math coaching for underserved students; funding and public activity effectively ceased in the early 2020s after failing to secure large-scale district or higher-ed adoption.
lesson: Grant- and small-round-funded AI tutoring companies targeting underserved populations often lack the capital runway to reach the scale needed for network effects in adaptive-learning data.
source: Crunchbase company status; SBIR/NSF grant records for Querium

name: Toppr
summary: Indian AI-adaptive test-prep platform acquired by Byju's in 2021 in an all-stock deal; suffered sharp declines in operations and staff cuts as its parent company's insolvency crisis unfolded from 2023 onward.
lesson: All-stock acquisitions tie a target company's fate entirely to the acquirer's financial health; Toppr's adaptive-learning technology could not be insulated from Byju's collapse.
source: Economic Times coverage of Byju's-Toppr integration and subsequent decline

name: Content Technologies Inc.
summary: AI startup (Cati) that used machine learning to auto-generate personalized textbooks and adaptive courseware from source material; pivoted toward enterprise AI content generation as its original education-publishing model failed to gain sustained traction.
lesson: Automating textbook creation solved a supply-side problem publishers didn't urgently feel; without demand-side pull from institutions, the technology had to be repurposed for a different buyer entirely.
source: Crunchbase; TechCrunch coverage of Content Technologies' pivot to enterprise AI

name: Cognii
summary: AI virtual-tutor and open-response grading engine using NLP to score short-answer questions and provide formative feedback; remained a small, thinly-funded player that never achieved the scale or funding of later NLP-grading entrants like Gradescope.
lesson: Being technologically early in NLP-based grading isn't sufficient if better-funded competitors later out-execute on go-to-market and integration with LMS ecosystems.
source: Crunchbase funding records; company website archive via Wayback Machine

name: Osmo
summary: AI-and-camera-based interactive learning system for young children (physical game pieces recognized via computer vision) acquired by Byju's in 2019; suffered inventory write-downs and business decline as Byju's overall financial distress deepened from 2022 onward.
lesson: Hardware-software AI-education products require sustained retail and manufacturing investment; when an acquirer's cash position deteriorates, capital-intensive hardware lines are often cut first.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Byju's-Osmo acquisition 2019 and later writedowns

name: Genius Education Group (Jingrui/Elite Education)
summary: Chinese AI-supported premium tutoring and adaptive learning group that listed on the Hong Kong exchange in 2021; filed for bankruptcy-style liquidation in 2022 after China's for-profit tutoring ban destroyed its core revenue base within months of its IPO.
lesson: Going public right before a regulatory shock is a worst-case timing scenario — public investors and the company itself absorbed the full impact of the policy change simultaneously.
source: Reuters/HKEX filings on Genius Education Group liquidation 2022

name: iTutorGroup
summary: AI-matched online English-tutoring company (brands including VIPABC and TutorABC) that became a landmark case when the U.S. EEOC sued it in 2022 for using AI hiring software that automatically rejected older female applicants, resulting in a $365,000 settlement — one of the first AI-discrimination enforcement actions in hiring.
lesson: Deploying AI screening tools without bias audits creates direct legal liability; regulators are increasingly willing to bring enforcement actions specifically targeting AI-driven discrimination.
source: EEOC v. iTutorGroup settlement press release, September 2023

name: TAL Education Group
summary: China's largest AI-driven after-school tutoring company (Xueersi brand, AI-adaptive learning products and 'MagicPen' hardware) lost over 90% of its market value and shut down its core K-9 academic tutoring business after the 2021 'double reduction' regulatory crackdown.
lesson: Even the largest incumbent in an AI-education vertical can be forced to abandon its entire core business line overnight when government policy targets the sector directly.
source: SEC filings (TAL); Reuters coverage of TAL business wind-down 2021

name: Carnegie Learning
summary: Pittsburgh-based pioneer of AI-driven adaptive math instruction (MATHia platform, spun out of Carnegie Mellon cognitive tutor research); built a durable K-12 and higher-ed business over two decades and was acquired by IXL Learning in 2024 in a strategic consolidation.
lesson: Deep academic research roots (CMU cognitive tutoring) combined with a patient, decades-long build in K-12 sales created a durable moat that newer, faster-burning adaptive-learning entrants could not replicate.
source: IXL Learning press release on Carnegie Learning acquisition, 2024

name: Third Space Learning
summary: UK-based one-to-one online tutoring platform that blends AI-driven lesson personalization and analytics with human tutors, scaling to reach hundreds of thousands of UK primary and secondary students, particularly in disadvantaged schools funded via the National Tutoring Programme.
lesson: Combining AI personalization with human tutors rather than replacing them entirely let Third Space Learning align with UK government tutoring-subsidy programs, creating a stable, policy-backed revenue base.
source: UK Companies House filings; EdTech Innovation Hub coverage of Third Space Learning growth

name: Riiid
summary: South Korean AI-tutoring company behind Santa, an AI test-prep app for English proficiency exams (TOEIC), which achieved unicorn status by demonstrating measurable score improvements and expanded into global AI-education infrastructure and generative-AI tutoring products.
lesson: Focusing AI adaptive learning on a single, high-stakes, measurable outcome (standardized test scores) made Riiid's value proposition easy to prove and market, accelerating trust and adoption.
source: Riiid Series D/E funding announcements; Korea JoongAng Daily coverage

name: Squirrel AI
summary: Chinese AI-adaptive learning company that built one of the world's largest deployments of intelligent tutoring systems (thousands of learning centers), using fine-grained knowledge-graph decomposition of subjects to personalize instruction at massive scale, largely for after-school and supplementary (non-banned) tutoring formats.
lesson: By focusing on B2B licensing of its adaptive-learning engine to schools and by operating in formats less directly targeted by China's 2021 crackdown, Squirrel AI diversified its exposure relative to peers that relied purely on for-profit after-school centers.
source: Forbes profile of Squirrel AI founder Derek Li; company funding announcements

name: Photomath
summary: Croatian-founded AI app that uses computer vision to scan and solve math problems with step-by-step explanations; grew to hundreds of millions of downloads and was acquired by Google in 2022 to integrate with Google Lens and broader education tools.
lesson: A narrow, extremely well-executed AI use case (camera-based math solving) with viral organic consumer distribution can build enough scale and strategic value to attract a top-tier acquirer.
source: Google official blog post announcing Photomath acquisition, 2022

name: DreamBox Learning
summary: Adaptive K-8 math and reading platform using algorithmic engines to adjust difficulty and pacing in real time based on granular student interaction data; built a large district footprint over 15 years and was acquired by Discovery Education in 2021.
lesson: Long-term, iterative investment in adaptive-algorithm accuracy and district-level trust building paid off with a strategic exit once the category consolidated.
source: Discovery Education press release on DreamBox acquisition, 2021

name: Newsela
summary: AI-powered platform that automatically rewrites news and nonfiction content at multiple reading levels for personalized literacy instruction, reaching a majority of U.S. school districts and expanding into broader AI-assisted formative assessment tools.
lesson: Solving a concrete, everyday teacher pain point (finding leveled reading content) with AI text-adaptation created immediate, tangible value that translated into wide district adoption.
source: Newsela Series D/E funding announcements; EdWeek Market Brief coverage

name: Amira Learning
summary: AI reading tutor using advanced speech-recognition tuned for children's voices to listen to students read aloud, assess fluency, and provide adaptive tutoring; scaled rapidly across U.S. school districts amid heightened post-pandemic literacy concerns.
lesson: Investing heavily in child-specific speech-recognition accuracy (rather than repurposing generic adult speech models) was the key technical differentiator that built district trust in a safety-sensitive literacy use case.
source: Amira Learning funding round coverage, EdSurge and Forbes

name: Gradescope
summary: AI-assisted grading platform originating from UC Berkeley research that uses machine learning to cluster and streamline grading of handwritten and coded assignments at scale; acquired by Turnitin in 2018 and became a widely used tool in higher-ed STEM courses.
lesson: A tightly scoped AI product solving a specific, high-pain faculty workflow (grading hundreds of exams) with modest capital needs can reach a profitable acquisition faster than broad adaptive-learning platforms.
source: Turnitin press release on Gradescope acquisition, 2018

name: Kira Talent
summary: AI-assisted video interview and applicant-assessment platform used by universities for admissions decisions; scaled across hundreds of graduate and professional school programs and was acquired by Kaplan in 2022.
lesson: Targeting a specific, budget-holding institutional buyer (admissions offices) with a clear ROI case (reduced interview logistics cost) built a sustainable enterprise SaaS business ahead of a strategic exit.
source: Kaplan press release on Kira Talent acquisition, 2022

name: ALEKS (Assessment and Learning in Knowledge Spaces)
summary: One of the earliest AI adaptive-learning systems, built on knowledge-space theory research from UC Irvine, used to assess and personalize math instruction; became a long-running commercial success and was acquired by McGraw-Hill in 2013, still widely used today.
lesson: Grounding an adaptive-learning algorithm in decades of peer-reviewed cognitive-science research produced a durable, defensible product that outlasted many later, less rigorously validated entrants.
source: McGraw-Hill Education press release on ALEKS acquisition, 2013

name: Magoosh
summary: AI-assisted, data-driven online test-prep platform for GRE, GMAT, SAT and other standardized exams, using adaptive practice and analytics to personalize study plans; built a profitable, largely bootstrapped business before being acquired by Nerdy Inc. in 2022.
lesson: A capital-efficient, profitability-first approach to AI-driven test prep let Magoosh reach a successful exit without needing the large funding rounds that left many competitors overextended.
source: Nerdy Inc. press release on Magoosh acquisition, 2022

name: Brainly
summary: Poland-founded peer-to-peer homework help platform that evolved into an AI-assisted learning app (Brainly AI tutor) serving hundreds of millions of students globally, reaching unicorn valuation in 2021, though it also conducted layoffs in 2023 amid broader market resets.
lesson: Building a large organic user base and knowledge-graph of student Q&A content before layering on generative AI gave Brainly a proprietary data advantage that pure AI-chatbot entrants lacked.
source: Brainly Series D/E funding announcements; TechCrunch coverage

name: Mathspace
summary: Australian AI-adaptive math platform that captures and analyzes students' step-by-step handwritten working (not just final answers) to give granular, personalized feedback; scaled across Australia, the UK, and the US school markets.
lesson: Capturing intermediate problem-solving steps rather than only final answers gave Mathspace's AI significantly richer diagnostic data, differentiating it from answer-only adaptive-learning competitors.
source: Mathspace funding announcements; AFR coverage of Mathspace growth

name: Realizeit
summary: Ireland-based adaptive learning platform provider used by universities and corporate training programs to power personalized courseware, sustaining steady growth as an infrastructure/API-based adaptive-learning provider to institutions and other edtech companies.
lesson: Positioning as B2B infrastructure that other education companies and universities license, rather than a consumer-facing brand, reduced customer-acquisition costs and marketing risk.
source: Realizeit company announcements; higher-ed procurement case studies

name: Cerego
summary: AI-driven spaced-repetition and adaptive memory platform (based on cognitive-science 'memory lift' algorithms) used by universities and corporate learning teams; sustained a long-running B2B licensing business, later rebranding parts of its technology as Fulcrum Labs for workforce training.
lesson: Applying rigorously validated cognitive-science principles (spaced repetition) to an AI engine gave Cerego a defensible, hard-to-replicate technical core distinct from generic content-recommendation systems.
source: Cerego company history; Fulcrum Labs rebrand announcements

name: Socratic
summary: AI-powered homework-help app that used computer vision and NLP to identify a photographed question and surface AI-curated explanations across subjects; acquired by Google in 2018 and later integrated into Google Lens and Search's education features.
lesson: Building an AI product with a clearly demonstrable, viral consumer use case (snap a photo, get an explained answer) made Socratic a natural acquisition target for a platform company seeking to embed AI education features.
source: Google official blog post announcing Socratic acquisition, 2018

name: Cuemath
summary: Indian AI-driven live math tutoring platform combining an adaptive curriculum engine with certified tutors, expanding internationally to the US, UK, and Southeast Asia and maintaining growth even as several Indian edtech peers contracted post-pandemic.
lesson: Focusing on a single subject (math) with a proprietary, research-backed adaptive curriculum allowed Cuemath to maintain differentiated quality and pricing power that broader, multi-subject competitors struggled to match.
source: Cuemath Series C/D funding announcements; Economic Times coverage

name: Physics Wallah
summary: Indian AI-assisted exam-prep platform (originating from a YouTube channel) that scaled to unicorn status by 2022 through extremely low-cost, high-volume test-prep courses with AI-driven doubt-solving and personalization, remaining profitable while many well-funded rivals burned cash.
lesson: Prioritizing affordability and capital efficiency over aggressive marketing spend let Physics Wallah reach unicorn status while staying profitable, in stark contrast to cash-burning competitors like Unacademy and Vedantu.
source: Economic Times/Entrackr coverage of Physics Wallah funding rounds

name: Testbook
summary: Indian AI-powered exam-preparation platform for government and competitive exams, using adaptive mock tests and analytics; achieved unicorn status in 2023 while maintaining a disciplined, largely profitable growth trajectory.
lesson: Deep specialization in a specific, high-demand vertical (government exam prep) with adaptive AI mock-testing let Testbook sustain profitability where more diversified competitors struggled.
source: Entrackr coverage of Testbook Series C and unicorn status, 2023

name: Embibe
funding: Acquired majority stake by Reliance Industries, 2018
summary: Indian AI-driven exam-prep and adaptive-learning platform that became majority-owned by Reliance Industries in 2018, giving it access to deep corporate backing and enabling sustained investment in its AI personalization engine for K-12 and competitive exam students.
lesson: Securing a strategic, deep-pocketed corporate parent (Reliance) rather than relying solely on venture rounds insulated Embibe from the funding-market volatility that hit venture-backed peers.
source: Reliance Industries press release on Embibe majority stake acquisition, 2018

name: Mainstay (AdmitHub)
summary: AI chatbot platform (originally AdmitHub, rebranded Mainstay) that helps colleges and universities proactively text-message and nudge prospective and enrolled students to improve enrollment yield and retention, scaling to hundreds of higher-ed institutional clients.
lesson: Targeting a measurable institutional KPI (enrollment yield and retention rates) with AI-driven nudging made ROI easy for university clients to quantify, supporting durable B2B contract renewals.
source: Mainstay company case studies; EdSurge coverage of AdmitHub rebrand

name: Aida (Aida School)
summary: Estonian AI-native math tutoring app using generative and adaptive AI to give real-time, step-by-step tutoring for students; acquired by European tutoring unicorn GoStudent in 2022 to bolster its AI-driven personalization capabilities.
lesson: A small, technically excellent AI-native team can achieve a successful strategic exit by being acquired for its core AI capability rather than needing to build an entire go-to-market operation independently.
source: GoStudent press release on Aida acquisition, 2022

name: Correlation One
summary: AI-driven workforce upskilling and adaptive-learning platform (data literacy and AI skills training) partnering with governments and corporations for large-scale reskilling programs; scaled steadily by aligning with public-sector and enterprise workforce-development budgets.
lesson: Anchoring an AI adaptive-learning product to government and enterprise workforce-development budgets, rather than consumer or school-district spend, provided larger and more stable contract sizes.
source: Correlation One Series C funding announcement, 2021; company government partnership case studies

name: Woot Math
summary: Adaptive fractions and rational-number math learning platform for K-8 built on NSF-funded research; achieved a successful strategic acquisition by Imagine Learning in 2019, continuing to operate as part of a larger adaptive-curriculum portfolio.
lesson: Deep focus on a single, well-researched conceptual weak spot (fractions) with strong efficacy data made Woot Math an attractive, de-risked acquisition target for a larger adaptive-curriculum platform.
source: Imagine Learning press release on Woot Math acquisition, 2019

name: Sana Labs
summary: Swedish AI adaptive-learning platform serving both corporate learning and education clients, using machine learning to personalize content sequencing and generate learning materials; raised growth-stage funding from top-tier investors including Bessemer and EQT.
lesson: Building a horizontal AI adaptive-learning engine usable across both corporate L&D and education verticals diversified Sana Labs' revenue base beyond any single, policy-sensitive market segment.
source: Sana Labs Series B/C funding announcements, TechCrunch coverage
